rationale: The citing paper uses Dietvorst et al. (2009) as a key prior study in its recommendation-neuroscience framing, not just as a passing background citation. It explicitly draws on Dietvorst et al.'s TPJ/mPFC mentalizing findings to motivate hypotheses about recommendation updating under peer feedback and to define recommendation-related ROI logic. The paper returns to Dietvorst et al. in the discussion to interpret its own TPJ results and position contributions relative to prior recommendation work.
relation_description: The citing study builds on Dietvorst et al. (2009) by extending salesperson mentalizing/recommendation-related neural insights to adolescent online word-of-mouth recommendation updating under social influence. Dietvorst et al. functions as a conceptual and empirical base for the citing paper's hypothesis framing and neural interpretation.
